item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like it, but it never really explains why the pathogen needs to be looked at to stay alive, which is kind of frustrating. How can it take over the world if it constantly needs to be stared at? How does looking away kill it and its host? Leaves me more frustrated than curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pleased you like it :)

Considering how easily this spreads, I needed a very remote origin point to justify why the whole world wasn't already infected. I originally thought about lost Amazon tribes, but that felt a bit too hackneyed, and the Sentinelese seemed a good alternative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. I've recently had a bit of a fascination with North Sentinel Island, and having something that originates from there is just pretty cool.

+1.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: This link is drawing a bit of a long bow, but I'm okay with it in the spirit of cross-linking.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

It was pointed out in the SCP-2218 discussion that the phenomena that takes place in that skip is similar to the effects of the SCP-2136 pathogen, so much so that I'd be willing to bet that at least one researcher at the Foundation hypothesizes that SCP-2218 instances are under the effect of a an altered version of the 2136 pathogen, or that 2136 is an altered version of the phenomena observed in 2218. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

Upon infection, the pathogen migrates to the host's motor neurons, producing an effect (SCP-2136-A) if the infected subject is out of the line-of-sight of another host. The characteristic symptoms of SCP-2136-A are a sudden stiffening of the unobserved subject's muscles, followed by complete immobility. Re-observation by another host during this period will reverse the symptoms, allowing the subject to regain muscle control. Death of an unobserved subject will occur within 5 minutes due to respiratory and circulatory failure.

To this:

Upon infection, the pathogen migrates to the host's motor neurons, producing an effect (SCP-2136-A) if the infected subject is out of the line-of-sight of another host. The characteristic symptoms of SCP-2136-A are a sudden stiffening of the unobserved subject's muscles, followed by complete immobility. Re-observation by another host during this period will reverse the symptoms, allowing the subject to regain muscle control. Death of an unobserved subject will occur within 5 minutes due to respiratory and circulatory failure1.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Similarity between the effects of SCP-2136 pathogen and SCP-2218 instances have been noted. Investigation into whether the anomalous properties affecting SCP-2218 instances are an altered version of SCP-2136, or visa versa, is ongoing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Description sets up the effect nicely, and then the logs hit it out of the park. That logs do an excellent job of building tension, and by the end of it I found myself clenching up in discomfort as I finished them. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pleased you enjoyed the presentation. As with many horror movies, it's no surprise as to what happens, so it's all about *how* it happens and how the characters react to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: From the standpoint of someone who's watched too many horror movies it was obvious they were infected, but the way it was presented was perfect. I should downvote for that name pun, but its too good. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi, author here. The WARNING box at the top is intended as an in-universe message, as if you're looking at the SCP article on your Foundation computer in the middle of editing.

The reason for using that approach is a bit spoiler-y to write here, but I have given both an in-universe spoiler, and an out-of-universe summary of why I used the format screw, on my author page. Jump to the SCP spoilers tab, scroll down to SCP-2136, and it's mostly covered by the first and last bullet points :)

Thanks for the +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 and all, but I'm still confused about the WARNING box at the top. I was trying to work out if this was intended as a collaborative article or something, but I assume I'm just missing something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks - glad you like it. I had basically written the whole thing before coming up with a title I liked. It was originally "Freeze tag", and then "At first sight", but I like the final title better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've read this on multiple occasions, and I just now got the pun in the title. "Driven." Excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so first-rate. +1

item.title: Re: Okay
item.content:encoded: Close. If you look at them, you can't move unless *they* look at *you*. Once you're infected, you always need another infected to look at you. Luckily, anyone looking at you gets infected, too!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like I read a draft for this absolutely ages ago. If my memory serves me correctly, I loved it then, and I still do. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These are the type of skips I love and are inspired by. Simple effect, and great execution on the horror aspect. I also think the use of the Sentinel Islands is a nice touch.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, shit, this is really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks Aleph. You are more than welcome to use any of the coding I have here - I have more or less stolen most of it myself.

If you're planning to use the tab structure of the journal, just be aware that it will make a mess of any other tabs in your sandbox. I eventually gave up and left that draft outside of tabview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks Kalinin. I agree with you that "LOOOK" is a bit OTT, but I wasn't sure how to make sure the ending was interpreted correctly without it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great! Simple, yet effective.

I just love how you organized the observation journal. (Mind if I steal the idea?)

Anyway. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "LOOOK" business at the end was just a touch goofy, but given the work put into the buildup prior, I can roll with it. Nice, entertaining read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Hints
- or PM me for utter spoilers

Blue text is generally used on the site to show revisions (particularly additions) in the same way as strike-through is used to show deletions.

The typos turn up again in the last three time entries on Day 5 of the journal. That's not a coincidence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get the typo-ridden blue text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops, not deliberate. I'd read a little about the Sentinel islands a while back, and they seemed a better alternative than "deepest darkest jungle" when thinking about an isolated location for the infection to start.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent job. It felt like a true creepypasta at the end (in a very good way). Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hot damn, looks like you (partially) used my challenge in the Bright Challenge Contest.

Or we both had an idea for a SCP involving North Sentinel Island…

Either way, gonna have to delete a SCP now. Still, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably for the best. Thank you for the cookie. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One internet cookie to TL333s! (and an honourable mention to MatthewtheLucky)

This skip did indeed originate from the idea of "what about a reverse -173?". An early conception would even have included the suggestion of cross-testing with 173, to see if we could make it immobile both when looked at and when not (with a supervisor's note warning that it might have precisely the opposite effect). In retrospect, I'm glad I didn't take that approach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks Will, and sorry for stealing the div - can't even remember from which article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the spot. This was one of the last sentences I wrote (based on forum feedback) and clearly I didn't quite parse it correctly. Appropriately for a naval perimeter, the missing word is "are".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Weeping Angel disease?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely one of the best SCPs I've read in a while. Hearty +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was thinking the blue text was just a format screw that would reveal the pathogen was taking people over and trying (poorly) to throw the Foundation off its scent. But this acquits itself nicely by the end. And I don't just mean the good horror in the journal (seriously, this is the perfect way to utilize the article format for horror): the effects are interesting, if somewhat similar to 173, and I had a blast reading up on the Sentinelese people.

Granted, if they're the ones from whence the pathogen originated, it can't be neutralized, can it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most of all, I really like the concept about the way this thing spreads. Introducing it at the start was a great way to hook me . And you used my div!

I should probably get to using it myself

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
MTFs Eta-10 and Theta-5 to establish a naval perimeter around the Sentinel Islands to prevent outside contact while a local containment protocol is determined.

Given the format screw, is there supposed to be a missing word here after Theta-5 and before "to"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvote that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Everyone should see this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And behold! There was much plus one-ing, and the site was well-pleased.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well done, indeed! Nice, gradual escalation to a satisfying payoff.

I totally want to call this Attention Whore Syndrome…

LOOOOOOOOOK

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is horror done right. Start with the basics. Hint at something more. Build the problem until the truth is revealed.

Easy +1.

ETA: And it makes the format screw info at the top mean so much more.

item.title: Okay
item.content:encoded:
It's kinda confusing, but here's what I can make of it; when you look at them, you can't move unless you look at them again. If you die, you can be looked at without issue.
+1?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for having a loook at this.

And many thanks to Eskobar and azzleflux for their input in the forums - much appreciated.

Edit: and a bonus internet cookie to anyone who can work out where the idea for this came from.
